subtopic_detail: PCOS first-line management in primary care
stem: A 24-year-old nulliparous woman presents to her GP with 9 months of secondary amenorrhoea, gradual weight gain and increased terminal hair growth on her chin and lower abdomen. She is not currently sexually active, has no plans for pregnancy in the next 2 years, and has no personal or family history of thromboembolism. Her cycles before this episode were always irregular at 6 to 10 weeks apart since menarche at age 14.

data_table.Examination: Mild acne on jawline; Ferriman-Gallwey score 11; no acanthosis; thyroid not palpable
data_table.Investigations: TSH, prolactin, 17-hydroxyprogesterone all within reference range; total testosterone 3.8 nmol/L; LH:FSH ratio 2.6; HbA1c 5.8%; TVS shows 14 antral follicles in one ovary, 16 in the other, ovarian volume 12 mL bilaterally

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Lifestyle modification targeting 5 to 10 percent weight loss plus a combined oral contraceptive for cycle regulation and androgen control
options.rationale: AU PCOS guidance recommends lifestyle as first line, with a COCP added for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism when fertility is not currently desired and there is no contraindication. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg on days 2 to 6 of an induced cycle to restore ovulation and reduce androgen burden
options.rationale: Ovulation induction is reserved for women actively trying to conceive; clomiphene does not address androgenic symptoms and is not appropriate when pregnancy is not desired. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

options.text: Spironolactone 100 mg daily as monotherapy to suppress androgens, with no other contraceptive cover added
options.rationale: Anti-androgens are teratogenic and must always be combined with reliable contraception in women of reproductive age. (Source: AMH: spironolactone)

options.text: Referral for laparoscopic ovarian drilling as a definitive surgical solution to the underlying ovarian pathology
options.rationale: Ovarian drilling is a second-line ovulation induction technique reserved for clomiphene-resistant women seeking pregnancy, not primary care management of PCOS. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

options.text: Empiric metformin 500 mg twice daily alone, without lifestyle counselling or contraceptive review at this visit
options.rationale: Metformin is an adjunct that may be added if cycle, weight or metabolic targets are not met with lifestyle; isolated metformin is not first-line and does not provide contraception or androgen control. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

explanation.summary: Australian-endorsed PCOS care begins with lifestyle change for any woman with elevated BMI, layered with a COCP when fertility is not currently desired to regulate cycles, oppose unopposed oestrogen and reduce androgenic symptoms. Anti-androgens such as spironolactone require reliable contraception because of teratogenicity. Metformin is an adjunct for metabolic or cycle goals not met with lifestyle alone. Ovulation induction agents are deferred until fertility is actively pursued.
key_points: Lifestyle and weight reduction of 5 to 10 percent improves cycle regularity and androgen profile in PCOS
COCP is first-line for cycle regulation and hyperandrogenism when pregnancy is not desired
Spironolactone must be co-prescribed with reliable contraception because of teratogenic risk
explanation.pearls: A combined oral contraceptive plus 5 to 10 percent weight loss over 6 months reduces free testosterone and re-establishes withdrawal bleeds in most women with PCOS without metabolic comorbidity.

sources.label: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG
